resources.string: Not connected to a Wi-Fi network
This network uses WPA3, the strongest available Wi-Fi encryption standard.
This network uses WPA2. It is adequate but can be vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks.
Consider upgrading your router to WPA3 if supported.
WPA is outdated and has known vulnerabilities including TKIP weaknesses.
Upgrade your router firmware and switch to WPA2 or WPA3.
WEP can be cracked in minutes with freely available tools. Your traffic is effectively unprotected.
Switch to WPA2 or WPA3 immediately. If your router only supports WEP, replace it.
This network has no encryption. Anyone nearby can intercept your traffic including passwords, emails, and browsing activity.
Avoid transmitting sensitive data. Use a VPN, or connect to a password-protected network.
Excellent signal strength. Connection should be stable and fast.
Good signal strength for normal use.
Signal is moderate. You may experience occasional slowdowns.
Move closer to the router or consider a Wi-Fi extender.
Weak signal increases packet loss and latency. Distant or spoofed access points often appear with low signal.
Move closer to the access point. Verify you are connected to the intended network.
This network does not broadcast its SSID. Hidden networks are not more secure — devices actively probe for them, which can leak the network name.
Hiding the SSID provides no real security. Rely on strong encryption (WPA3/WPA2) instead.
Wi-Fi Protected Setup (WPS) has a known brute-force vulnerability that can expose your network password.
Disable WPS in your router settings. Use a strong password with WPA2/WPA3 instead.
The router supports both TKIP and AES/CCMP. TKIP is a legacy cipher with known weaknesses.
Configure your router to use AES/CCMP only and disable TKIP.
This network uses only TKIP, a deprecated cipher vulnerable to certain attacks.
Switch your router to AES/CCMP mode. Most devices made after 2010 support it.
You are on the 2.4 GHz band, which is more congested and susceptible to interference from neighboring networks and devices.
Connect to the 5 GHz band if your router offers it for better speed and less interference.
The negotiated link speed is very low, which may indicate an old access point or heavy interference.
Check for sources of interference. Consider upgrading to a newer router.
